topic: Food Safety, Food Science and Special Diets
subject: food-technology

items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Why must the 14 major allergens be highlighted on UK food labels?
items.correct: Reactions can be severe or life-threatening, so consumers must identify them at a glance.
items.misconception: To advertise that the food contains them.
items.why: Students miss the consumer-safety logic. Labelling laws require allergens to be visibly listed (bold/highlighted) so allergic people can quickly check. Penalties for non-compliance can be severe.

items.q: Are lactose intolerance and milk allergy the same condition?
items.correct: No - lactose intolerance is a digestive problem (lack of the lactase enzyme); milk allergy is an immune reaction to milk protein and can be severe.
items.misconception: Yes - they are just two names for not getting on with milk.
items.random_wrong: Both are caused by the calcium in milk.
items.why: Mechanism matters. Intolerance = missing lactase enzyme, so undigested lactose ferments in the gut (bloating, discomfort) - unpleasant but not dangerous. Allergy = immune system attacks milk PROTEINS (e.g. casein), with reactions up to anaphylaxis. Management differs too.

items.q: Is coeliac disease a food allergy?
items.correct: No - coeliac disease is an autoimmune condition where gluten triggers the immune system to damage the gut lining.
items.misconception: Yes - coeliac disease is simply a gluten allergy.
items.random_wrong: Coeliac disease is caused by eating too much sugar.
items.why: Often mislabelled an allergy. In coeliac disease, gluten causes the body to attack its own small-intestine lining, flattening the villi and impairing nutrient absorption. It is lifelong, diagnosed medically, and requires strict gluten avoidance - not an IgE allergic reaction.
It's Gluten-free. “Vegan” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Number of major animal-product categories vegans avoid (meat, fish, dairy, eggs, honey: pick number)?
items.why: The real figure is 5 categories. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
